text: DEVELOPMENT OF A MODEL FOR COMPUTING $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ MIGRATION IN THE MSRE
Theoretical Mass Transfer Coefficients 71

section: DEVELOPMENT OF A MODEL FOR COMPUTING $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ MIGRATION IN THE MSRE
text: DEVELOPMENT OF A MODEL FOR COMPUTING $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ MIGRATION IN THE MSRE
Because of the importance of neutron economy to the breeder concept, it is necessary to know the dynamics of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ in the circulating-fuel system.
Since $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ in the core impairs the neutron economy, it is important to understand the mass transfer mechanism involved and the parameters that may be varied to control it.
This report deals primarily with developing a model for computing the migration of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ in the MSRE and with experiments conducted to establish the model.
A preoperational experiment was run in the MSRE with $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ tracer, and many of the gas-transport constants were inferred from the results.
Equivalent transport constants for calculating the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ migration gave a poisoning of about $1.4\%$ without circulating bubbles and well below $1\%$ with bubbles.
Preliminary measurements made on the critical reactor show xenon poisoning of 0.3 to $0.4\%$ .
Nuclear poisons, notably $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ , can detract significantly from the breeding potential.
The $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning is a function of the steady-state $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ concentration in the reactor core.
This report is concerned principally with developing a model for estimating the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning in the MSRE.
The normal operating temperature is $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and the thermal power level is 7.5 Mw.
1, and pertinent design parameters are listed in Appendix A.
Figure 2 shows details of the fuel pump.
It is rated for $1200\mathrm{gpm}$ at a 48.5-ft head.
The normal operating helium pressure in the pump bowl is 5 psig, which is also the pump suction pressure.
about 50 gpm, but it has not been measured directly.
In addition to the stripper there is salt flow of about 15 gpm from behind the impeller, through a labyrinth along the shaft, and into the pump bowl.
The principal helium flow through the pump bowl is 2.4 std liters/min purge down the shaft to prevent radioactive gases and salt mist from reaching the bearing region of the pump.
There is an additional helium flow of 0.9 std liters/min
Figure 3 is an isometric view of the reactor core.
contact with the fuel salt, and therefore $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ may diffuse into its porous structure; the graphite acts as a $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ concentrator in the core.

The theoretical void percentage of MSRE graphite (grade CGB) is $17.7\%$ , and slightly over half of it is accessible to a gas such as xenon. $^2$ Other pertinent properties of this graphite are listed in Appendix A, and more detailed information is available in Refs.
The rate at which $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ diffuses to the graphite is a function of the salt-to-graphite mass transfer coefficient, which is, in turn, a function of the fuel-salt Reynolds number.
First there is the bulk of the graphite ( $\sim 95\%$ ), which is characterized by salt velocities of about 0.7 ft/sec and a Reynolds number of about 1000.
The second fluid dynamic region is composed of the centermost channels in the core (about 18).
They do not have orificing grid bars below them, so the fuel velocities are higher, about 1.8 ft/sec, and give a Reynolds number of about 2500.
This region comprises about $1.5\%$ of the graphite and is in a zone of high nuclear importance.
These grid bars are subject to high salt velocities, a maximum of about 5 ft/sec, and comprise a fluid dynamic entrance region.
This region is not too well defined but probably comprises about $3.5\%$ of the total graphite.

Xenon-135 poisoning in the MSRE was considered previously,[5-7] but these calculations were generally of an approximate design nature because of lack of information on the values of the rate constants involved.
In order to calculate the steady-state $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning in the reactor, it was first necessary to compute the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ concentration dissolved in the salt.
The most significant $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ source term is that which comes from the decay of $^{135}\mathrm{I}$ ; in addition a small amount is produced directly from fission.

The stripping efficiency of the pump bowl spray ring was measured at the University of Tennessee as part of a masters degree thesis. $^{8,9}$ This work was done with a $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ -water system maintained bubble free and later confirmed with and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ -water system, also maintained bubble free.
Xenon-135 burnup and decay rates are relatively well known.
The first phase was an addition phase and consisted of adding $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ to one of the pump-bowl level-indicator bubbler lines at a steady rate for a period of time.

During this phase the pump bowl reached some equilibrium $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ concentration almost immediately; then the salt dissolved krypton via the xenon-stripper spray ring; and the graphite absorbed krypton from the salt.
Krypton-85 was chosen for the experiment primarily for ease of continuous monitoring at low concentrations in the off-gas line; also its low cost and availability were considerations.
Figure 5 is a schematic diagram of the krypton experiment facilities.
The addition station controls the flow of an $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ -He mixture into bubbler line 593.
The normal bubbler flow of pure helium (0.37 std liters/min) was maintained to transport the krypton-helium mixture into the pump bowl.
The krypton-helium container was made from 12-in. sched.-80 carbon steel pipe and pipe caps and was about 5 ft long.
It was hydrostatically tested at 520 psig.
On one end was a U-tube and valve arrangement that was used to transfer $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ from its shipping container to the experiment container.
This resulted in about $95\%$ transfer.
This two-step process resulted in the almost perfect transfer of the 120 curies of $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ purchased.
The experiment container was then pressurized to 180 psig with helium.
After the first run it was further pressurized with helium to 275 psig.
The original 120 curies of $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ amounted to only about two liters, and this had to be added continuously to the reactor for a period of several days.
It consisted of an 8-in. aluminum ball inside the tank that rolled back and forth as the tank was rocked.
A large coil of $1/4$ -in. stainless steel tubing was located between the krypton-helium container and flow control equipment to compensate for the rocking motion.
The limiting flow valve was set to limit the flow from the container to about 20 std liters/hr in case of a complete rup-ture downstream.
The remainder of the flow control system consisted of conventional filters (5 to $9\mu$ ), pressure gages, and low-capacity valves.
5, all the reactor off-gas from the pump bowl went through the monitoring station.
3 $\sim 100\mathrm{mg / cm}^2$ plastic window $(7.62\times 2.54~\mathrm{cm})$ in $5.9\mathrm{g / cm}^2$ brass container
The four GM tubes were suspended in a 2-liter stainless steel laboratory beaker.
The monitors were calibrated with small samples of $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ .
2 absorbed $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ and gave a false count rate; also it affected other tubes in the array.
2 became identical with tube No.
Much consideration was given to the safety aspect of handling 120 curies of $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ .
The half-life of $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ is 10.3 y and it gives off 0.695- and 0.15-Mev beta particles and a 0.54-Mev gamma ray.
The daughter product is $^{85}\mathrm{Rb}$ , which is stable.
A continuous flow of air (17,000 to 20,000 cfm) was maintained through the reactor building and released to the atmosphere through a 100-ft stack.

Detailed procedures for transferring $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ , pressurizing the container, and conducting the addition and stripping phases of the experiment were written and approved by appropriate personnel.

The procedure used to start the addition phase was to adjust the krypton-helium container regulator so that the pressure gage just upstream of the main flow control valve was about 10 psig, that is, about 5 psi over the pump bowl pressure.
During the addition phase the system was checked every $\frac{1}{2}$ to 1 hr, and the flow control was adjusted as necessary to maintain a constant activity in the off-gas line.
The krypton-helium container was agitated for about 15 min every 2 to 4 hr.
For various runs the krypton-helium injection rate ranged from 2 to 6.3 std liters/hr but was held constant for each run.
At the start of the stripping phase, a 1-min count was taken every 1 l/2 min.
The times gradually increased until at the end of the long runs (2 and 3) a 1/2-hr count was taken every hour.
1 and 2 that there are two essentially stagnant lines entering the pump bowl, the sampler-enricher line and the overflow line.
These lines were purged free of $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ before the stripping phase started and at various times during the stripping operation.
Six $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ addition and stripping runs were made.
Table 1 summarizes the operational parameters in these runs.
Figures 6 through 11 show the results of these runs.
No correction was necessary for the decay of $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ because its half-life is so long compared with the time scale of each run.
As pointed out previously, the data from run 1 are erroneous because of $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ absorbed on the plastic shielding a GM tube 2.
In all cases after run 1 the background count for tubes 3 and 4 was less than 15 cpm.

This procedure for run 3 resulted in five exponentials, which is somewhat significant because of the five major stripping operations (pump bowl, salt, including bubbles, and three graphite regions).
Figure 14 shows the results of this exponential peeling process on run 3.

The rate constants are a function only of the slopes of the exponentials involved, so absolute calibrations of the monitor and detailed knowledge of the $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ concentrations are not necessary.
Numerical results of peeling run 3 and their interpretation are given in Table 2.

In the first term $C_{g}^{k}$ is the mean $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ concentration in the gas phase of the pump bowl and the product $EC_{g}^{k}$ is the concentration in the off-gas line; therefore, $E$ can be thought of as a mixing efficiency.
If these three terms are evaluated at the beginning of the stripping phase, the second term is approximately $\frac{1}{500}$ of the first term, so it can be neglected.
The third term is about $\frac{1}{20}$ or less of the first term for expected values of $\psi$ and $S'$ .
The above equation also neglects the $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ contribution by the stagnant kidney in the bubbler line semitoroid, but estimates indicate that this is also an adequate assumption.

Note that the half-life measured in the monitor at about $70^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is identical to the reactor half-lives where the operating temperature is $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Runs 1 through 6 gave the results listed in Table 3.
The average pump bowl purging efficiency is $69\%$ , and it is not a strong function of pump bowl level.
As pointed out previously, values for the stripping efficiency could not be extracted from the data with any degree of accuracy, even though runs 4, 5, and 6 were performed with this goal in mind.

Very rough calculations do indicate that the stripping efficiencies are more or less consistent with those measured at the University of Tennessee in a $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ -water system, but the calculations are so approximate and dependent on hazy assumptions that they will not be presented here.
bulk graphite region ( $\sim 95\%$ ) that is characterized by salt velocities of about 0.7 ft/sec and a Reynolds number of about 1000.
Second, there is the graphite associated with the centermost fuel channels, which comprises about $1.5\%$ of the graphite.
It is difficult to determine the exact boundary of this region, but it probably consists of approximately $3.5\%$ of the graphite and is in a zone of low nuclear importance.

The rate at which $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ is leached from the graphite is a function of several parameters; for instance, the diffusivity of krypton in graphite, the mass transfer coefficient, and the $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ concentration dissolved in the bulk salt, which is in turn a function of the xenon stripper efficiency.
Most of the calculations will be confined to run 3, which was concerned primarily with measuring graphite rate constants.

Consider the $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ flux from graphite as a function only of its internal resistance $\left(\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{G}}^{\mathrm{k}}\right)$ and its external resistance $\left(\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{k}}\right)$ .

Considering run 3 it is obvious that the slowest exponent $(t_{1/2} = 198\, \text{hr})$ is related to the bulk graphite, and it is expected that the next exponential $(t_{1/2} = 15.5\, \text{hr})$ is related to the graphite region located at the center line of the core.
The next exponential $(t_{1/2} = 4.52\, \text{hr})$ has a fairly low order of confidence in equating it to any specific graphite region and will not be considered.

It is difficult to extract $h_m^k$ information from run 2 because the time intervals involved were too short.
The krypton addition and stripping phases were about 60 hr each.
During this relatively short addition time the bulk graphite reached only about $20\%$ of its saturated value, in contrast to run 3, where it reached about $70\%$ of its saturated value.

After stripping for 60 hr in run 2, the slope of the flux curve is not determined by a single graphite region but is still under the influence of two graphite regions, and it cannot be peeled by the same technique as run 3.
Nevertheless, run 2 was looked at, and without presenting any results, it will be stated that it was consistent with run 3.

Nevertheless, it seems that a reasonable range of $h_m^{kCL}$ for the MSRE would be between 0.25 and 0.4 ft/hr.
For instance, the integral of the bulk graphite curve $(t_{1/2} = 198\mathrm{hr})$ should yield the approximate $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ capacity of this graphite.
Then, from knowledge of the $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ addition concentration, we could compute the approximate graphite void fraction available to krypton.
When this calculation was performed with the addition concentration taken as the mean pump bowl concentration, the graphite void fraction came out to be about 0.40.

Now, if we compute the graphite void fraction based on the dissolved krypton in the salt being in equilibrium with the bubbler addition stream, the result is about 0.04, and this is lower than would be expected.

It should be pointed out that the choice of a bubbler line for $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ addition as opposed to the pressure reference leg, which would not have given this deleterious effect, was dictated by other considerations.

section: Dissolved Xenon Source Terms and Considerations
text: The $^{135}\mathrm{I}$ generation rate is $6.1\%$ per fission, either direct or from the decay of $^{135}\mathrm{Te}$ .

section: Dissolved Xenon Source Terms and Considerations
text: It in turn decays to $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ with a 6.68-hr half-life.

section: Dissolved Xenon Source Terms and Considerations
text: The total $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ generation rate is therefore $6.1 + 0.3 = 6.4\%$ per fission, and, as will be seen from the next consideration, is confined completely to the salt phase.

section: Dissolved Xenon Source Terms and Considerations
text: Since $^{135}\mathrm{I}$ and $^{135}\mathrm{Te}$ are precursors of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ , their chemistry is important.

section: Dissolved Xenon Source Terms and Considerations
text: Concerning iodine, its thermodynamic properties indicate this to be true. $^{10}$ Recent evidence from the reactor also indicates this to be true.

section: Dissolved Xenon Source Terms and Considerations
text: Some iodine has been found in the off-gas system (but very little, if any, $^{135}\mathrm{I}$ ), but this is due to the volatilization of the precursor tellurium.

section: Dissolved Xenon Source Terms and Considerations
text: Since $^{135}\mathrm{Te}$ has such a short half-life ( $< 0.5\mathrm{min}$ ), very little of it will have a chance to volatilize; therefore this effect is neglected.

section: Dissolved Xenon Sink Terms and Considerations
text: Xenon-135 decays with a half-life of 9.15 hr, and decay takes place throughout the entire fuel loop.

section: Dissolved Xenon Sink Terms and Considerations
text: Xenon-135 has a neutron absorption cross section of $1.18 \times 10^{6}$ barns averaged over the MSRE neutron spectrum.[11]

section: Dissolved Xenon Sink Terms and Considerations
text: As noted earlier, the efficiency of the xenon stripper was measured at the University of Tennessee with a $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ -water system and confirmed later with an $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ -water system.

section: Dissolved Xenon Sink Terms and Considerations
text: In magnitude it turns out to be between 8 and $15\%$ .

section: Dissolved Xenon Sink Terms and Considerations
text: Diffusion experiments² with a single sample of CGB graphite yielded a diffusion coefficient of xenon in helium at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 20 psig of about $2.4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$ ( $9.2 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{ft}^2/\mathrm{hr}$ ).
The total $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ yield is $6.4\%$ per fission.
The corresponding $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ generation rate is $5.44 \times 10^{19}$ $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ atoms per hour at $7.5\mathrm{Mw}$ .

in units of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ atoms/hr. ft $^2$ .
Because of the flux distributions, graphite distributions, and the various fluid dynamic regions, the core will be handled incrementally as the 72 regions described earlier.
We can now solve for the total $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ flux into the graphite, as follows:

The salt film is by far the controlling resistance; therefore the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ concentration in the bubble is uniform and at equilibrium with the concentration in the salt at the interface.

The xenon poisoning as obtained in this report is defined as the number of neutrons absorbed by $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ over the number of neutrons (fast and thermal) absorbed by $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and weighted according to neutron importance; it is expressed as a percentage.

\sigma^ {x} \phi_ {2 e F _ {e} V _ {e} \overline {{C}} _ {G} ^ {x}} = \frac {h _ {m e} ^ {x} Y V _ {e} F _ {e} C _ {s} ^ {x}}{1 + \frac {h _ {m e} ^ {x} H R T}{\beta_ {e} D _ {G} ^ {x}} \frac {I _ {0} (\beta_ {e} r _ {1})}{I _ {1} (\beta_ {e} r _ {1})}} \left(\frac {\phi_ {2 e} \sigma^ {x}}{\phi_ {2 e} \sigma^ {x} + \lambda^ {x}}\right).
With this substitution the poisoning of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ in the MSRE can be computed.

Predicted $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ Poison Fraction in the MSRE Without Circulating Bubbles at 7.5 Mw(t).
Predicted $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ Poison Fraction in the MSRE Without Circulating Bubbles at 7.5 Mw(t).

A simple calculation would show that with a circulating void fraction of 0.01 and the xenon in the liquid and gas phases in equilibrium, about $98\%$ of the xenon present would be in the bubbles, or if the void fraction is 0.001, $83\%$ of the xenon would be in the bubbles.
We would therefore expect that a small amount of circulating helium bubbles would have a pronounced effect on $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning.

The most significant indications come from "sudden pressure release tests." These experiments consist of slowly increasing the system pressure from 5 to 15 psig and then suddenly venting the pressure off.
Void fractions can be computed from these tests that range from 0 to 0.03 but are generally less than 0.01.

The bubbles that migrated from the pump bowl into the pump suction could be seen and were about the size of a "pinpoint." For lack of any better measurement they were taken to be in the order of 0.010 in. in diameter.
16 through 19 and other sources, the mass transfer coefficient was estimated to be in the range 1 to 4 ft/hr and practically independent of diameter.
This is defined as the percentage of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ enriched bubbles that burst in passing through the spray ring and are replaced with pure helium bubbles.
For lack of any better information, a nominal value was taken as $10\%$ , because this is about the salt stripping efficiency, but it could just as easily be in the order of $100\%$ .

Figures 21 and 22 show the computed $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning as a function of circulating void volume with other parameters ranging as indicated.
Predicted $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ Poison Fraction in the MSRE with Circulating Bubbles at 7.5 Mw(t).
Predicted $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ Poison Fraction in the MSRE with Circulating Bubbles at 7.5 Mw(t).
Circulating bubbles have a very pronounced effect on $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning, even at very low void percentages.
The $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning is a rather weak function of the bubble mass transfer coefficient and diameter over the expected range.
At the time this report was written, there was no accurate knowledge of the extent of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning.
Preliminary values based on reactivity balances indicate it is in the range 0.3 to $0.4\%$ .

Actually, the most recent information seems to indicate that the circulating void fraction $(\psi)$ is in the order of 0.1 to $0.3\%$ and the bubble stripping efficiency $(S')$ is in the range of 50 to $100\%$ .
Equipment is currently being built to measure $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poison fractions more accurately.
A transient experiment such as the $^{85}\mathrm{Kr}$ experiment can be useful in determining rate constants and other information for a complex process such as noble gas dynamics in the MSRE.
If the MSRE could be operated bubble free, the computed $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning would be 1.3 to $1.5\%$ at 7.5 Mw.

When the model is modified to include circulating bubbles, the computed values can be made to agree with preliminary measured values $(0.3 - 0.4\%)$ by adjusting bubble parameters used over reasonably expected ranges.
However, to prove this conclusively, we must have accurate knowledge of the bubble parameters, and with these calculate precisely the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ poisoning.
The circulating helium bubble concept should be considered seriously as a $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ removal mechanism in future molten-salt reactors.

The mean pore entrance diameter for CGB graphite is less than $0.1\mu$ and probably closer to $0.02\mu$ , which is extremely small compared with the boundary layer thickness.

Associated with each pore is a unit transfer cell of cross-sectional area $\pi r_f^2$ , through which krypton is transferred by conventional mass transfer from the position of $C_{sf}^k$ to the bulk salt at concentration $C_s^k$ .
